name: Cuban Exiles JFK
description: The 'Cuban Exiles JFK' theory posits that anti-Castro Cuban exiles, possibly in conjunction with the CIA and/or the Mafia, were involved in the assassination of President John F. Kennedy. This theory arises from the Bay of Pigs fiasco and Kennedy's subsequent reluctance to provide further military support to the exiles, leading to deep resentment and a perceived betrayal. Proponents suggest these exiles had the motive, means, and opportunity to participate in the assassination. They argue that Kennedy's policies directly undermined their efforts to overthrow Castro, damaging their financial interests and personal vendettas. Evidence cited often includes alleged connections between Lee Harvey Oswald and anti-Castro groups, suspicious activities by exiles in the lead-up to the assassination, and perceived inconsistencies in the official Warren Commission report. Mainstream historical analysis generally dismisses the Cuban exiles theory as lacking concrete evidence. Historians point out that while some exiles certainly harbored resentment towards Kennedy, there is no credible proof linking them directly to the assassination. The Warren Commission concluded that Oswald acted alone, and subsequent investigations have found no definitive evidence to contradict this finding. Many researchers argue that the focus on Cuban exiles distracts from other potential avenues of inquiry and relies on circumstantial evidence and speculation.
subtitle: Alleged involvement in the JFK assassination.

claims.claim: Cuban exiles had the motive to assassinate JFK due to his perceived betrayal after the Bay of Pigs.
claims.evidence: Resentment expressed by some exiles, loss of financial interests, and perceived lack of support for their anti-Castro efforts.
claims.criticism: While resentment existed, it doesn't equate to direct involvement in the assassination. No irrefutable evidence links specific exiles to the crime.

claims.claim: Lee Harvey Oswald had connections to anti-Castro Cuban groups or individuals.
claims.evidence: Alleged sightings of Oswald with exiles, claims of him providing support or information to them.
claims.criticism: These connections are largely unsubstantiated and often based on unreliable eyewitness accounts. No concrete proof demonstrates Oswald's active collaboration with exiles in a plot to kill Kennedy.

claims.claim: Certain Cuban exiles exhibited suspicious behavior before and after the assassination, suggesting their involvement.
claims.evidence: Unexplained movements, alleged coded messages, and attempts to conceal information.
claims.criticism: Such behaviors are often interpreted subjectively and lack definitive proof of criminal intent. They can be attributed to a variety of factors unrelated to the assassination.

keyFigures.name: Antonio Veciana
keyFigures.role: Claimed to have witnessed Oswald meeting with a CIA agent and anti-Castro exile.

keyFigures.name: G. Robert Blakey
keyFigures.role: Chief Counsel and Staff Director for the House Select Committee on Assassinations (HSCA).

keyFigures.name: Gaeton Fonzi
keyFigures.role: Investigator for the HSCA who pursued leads related to Cuban exiles.

timeline.event: Bay of Pigs Invasion fails, leading to resentment among Cuban exiles.
Cuban Missile Crisis further strains relations between Kennedy and some Cuban exiles.
John F. Kennedy is assassinated in Dallas, Texas.
The Warren Commission concludes that Lee Harvey Oswald acted alone.
The House Select Committee on Assassinations (HSCA) concludes that there was probably a conspiracy in the Kennedy assassination but does not definitively implicate Cuban exiles.

sources.author: G. Robert Blakey and Richard Billings
sources.work: The Plot to Kill the President
sources.title: G. Robert Blakey and Richard Billings

sources.author: Gerald Posner
sources.work: Case Closed: Lee Harvey Oswald and the Assassination of JFK
sources.title: Gerald Posner
